case_name: Crisuron removal from surface water for drinking water production with minimal chemical use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Diuron-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under a minimal-chemical-use preference.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal waterworks draws from a lowland river reservoir affected by seasonal agricultural runoff, and monitoring has identified Crisuron as the primary organic micropollutant of concern. The plant already uses standard solids removal and disinfection, but operators want an upgrade path that minimizes additional chemical consumption while reliably producing drinking water. Natural organic matter and moderate turbidity are present, so matrix effects on adsorption and oxidation performance must be considered.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable micropollutant control for Diuron in compliance with drinking water expectations while maintaining conventional microbiological safety and acceptable finished-water quality.
secondary_goals: use established and robust treatment technology
limit energy demand to a moderate level
maintain manageable operator burden under variable surface-water conditions

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: backwash water and spent carbon handling are manageable, but large increases in chemical sludge production are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable performance is required during seasonal runoff and DOC fluctuations

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove debris and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.clarification_or_direct_filtration: reduce turbidity and particulate natural organic matter to improve downstream adsorption performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb Diuron and provide polishing for dissolved organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: Diuron is a hydrophobic neutral pesticide that is commonly controlled with activated carbon in drinking-water applications
surface water has moderate DOC and turbidity, so front-end solids reduction helps preserve carbon capacity and improve reliability
the minimal-chemical-use preference strongly favors adsorption over high-dose chemical oxidation or coagulation-intensive solutions
GAC is an established and robust option for municipal surface-water polishing with manageable operator complexity
potential_risks: DOC competition can shorten GAC bed life and increase replacement or regeneration frequency
insufficient pretreatment may cause headloss buildup and reduced adsorption efficiency
breakthrough monitoring is needed because Diuron removal declines as carbon exhausts

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse debris and stabilize raw-water intake conditions
core_unit_functions.clarification: remove PAC-associated solids and suspended matter
core_unit_functions.filtration: polish remaining particles and PAC carryover

applicability_rationale: PAC is a proven conventional response to episodic pesticide contamination in surface waters
it can be flexibly dosed during seasonal Crisuron peaks rather than operated continuously
the train is familiar to many surface-water plants and compatible with conventional clarification and filtration
it ranks below GAC because it requires routine chemical addition and can increase solids handling
potential_risks: higher PAC demand may occur under elevated DOC conditions, reducing cost-effectiveness
performance depends on contact time, dose control, and mixing quality
additional sludge production conflicts somewhat with the minimal-chemical-use preference

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse materials and protect equipment
core_unit_functions.clarification: lower particle load and reduce oxidant demand from suspended solids
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize Diuron and transform dissolved organic matter into more biodegradable fractions
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual organics and biologically polish ozonation byproducts

applicability_rationale: ozonation followed by BAC is an established advanced treatment approach for surface-water micropollutants
BAC can reduce residual biodegradable oxidation products and extend carbon usefulness
this train is technically strong for pesticide-affected surface waters with organic matrix effects
it ranks below plain GAC and PAC because it uses more energy and adds operational complexity despite limited direct chemical dosing
potential_risks: ozone dose must be carefully controlled because NOM can consume oxidant and alter micropollutant conversion efficiency
bromate formation may be a concern if bromide is present and is not well controlled
biological filters require stable operation and monitoring to avoid performance instability

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: protect membrane systems from coarse solids
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: remove suspended solids, algae, and pathogens but not dissolved Diuron
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: remove dissolved Diuron and residual taste-and-odor organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial barrier and distribution protection

applicability_rationale: UF provides a robust front-end barrier for variable surface water and can stabilize downstream GAC loading
GAC remains the main Diuron removal step because UF alone does not reject small dissolved pesticide molecules effectively
this option is feasible where a membrane-based particulate barrier is desired
it ranks below conventional pretreatment plus GAC because it generally has higher capital and energy needs for a case driven mainly by one pesticide
potential_risks: membrane fouling can increase cleaning frequency and operational burden
chemical cleaning and membrane replacement add lifecycle cost
if GAC monitoring is weak, dissolved Diuron breakthrough can still occur despite excellent particulate control

core_unit_functions.intake_screening: remove coarse debris before high-pressure treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of dissolved organics including Diuron
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished-water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes are technically capable of reducing Diuron to very low levels
they may be considered where multiple dissolved contaminants must be simultaneously removed
for this case they are not the preferred solution because the goal is straightforward pesticide control under moderate constraints with minimal unnecessary complexity
potential_risks: concentrate management creates a significant disposal burden
high energy demand conflicts with the stated moderate operating constraints
pretreatment and scaling control increase operational complexity beyond what is needed for a Diuron-focused case

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_trains should rank at the top for Diuron in drinking-water surface sources
GAC_or_PAC_with_conventional_solids_removal should outrank high-pressure membrane options under minimal_chemical_use and moderate constraints
membrane_only_or_high_energy options should not rank first for a single primary pesticide case

must_reflect: minimal additional chemical use should favor GAC and penalize coagulation-intensive or oxidation-intensive options unless clearly justified
moderate energy and operator capability should support established municipal adsorption solutions
surface-water variability should be acknowledged through pretreatment and robustness considerations

must_contain: the role of activated carbon as a mainstream control method for pesticide-like organic micropollutants such as Diuron
the influence of DOC and turbidity on adsorption performance and bed life
why UF alone is insufficient for dissolved Diuron removal
why NF_or_RO rank lower despite strong removal capability

should_mention: ozonation byproduct concerns such as bromate if oxidation is proposed
